([Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 字; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *zì*; lit. 'character'), also known as a **style name**, is an additional name bestowed upon individuals at adulthood, complementing their given name.[[1]](#cite_note-Wilkinson-1) This tradition is prevalent in the [East Asian cultural sphere](/wiki/East_Asian_cultural_sphere "East Asian cultural sphere"), particularly in [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Courtesy names are a marker of adulthood and were historically given to men at the age of 20, and sometimes to women upon marriage.
Ulrich Theobald. [Names of Persons and Titles of Rulers](http://www.chinaknowledge.de/History/Terms/titles.html)

["Qū lǐ shàng" 曲禮上](https://ctext.org/liji/qu-li-i#n9516) [Summary of the Rules of Propriety Part 1]. *Lǐjì* 禮記 [*[Book of Rites](/wiki/Book_of_Rites "Book of Rites")*]. Line 44. "A son at twenty is capped, and receives his appellation....When a daughter is promised in marriage, she assumes the hair-pin, and receives her appellation."
["Language Log » Sun Yat-sen, Chiang Kai-shek, Mao Zedong"](https://languagelog.ldc.upenn.edu/nll/?p=53453). Retrieved 2024-08-05.
*(孫破虜吳夫人，吳主權母也。 ... 與弟景居。 ... 生四男一女。) Sanguozhi vol. 50*.

Adamek, Piotr (2017). *A Good Son is Sad If He Hears the Name of His Father: The Tabooing of Names in China as a Way of Implementing Social Values*. London: Routledge. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780367596712](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780367596712 "Special:BookSources/9780367596712").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Name given to adults in East Asia

For European nobility, see [courtesy title](/wiki/Courtesy_title "Courtesy title").

| --- | --- |

|  | |

| [Chữ Nôm](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m "Chữ Nôm") | 𠸜字   𠸜𡨸 |

| [McCune–Reischauer](/wiki/McCune%E2%80%93Reischauer "McCune–Reischauer") | *cha* |

|  | |

A **courtesy name** ([Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 字; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *zì*; lit. 'character'), also known as a **style name**, is an additional name bestowed upon individuals at adulthood, complementing their given name.[[1]](#cite_note-Wilkinson-1) This tradition is prevalent in the [East Asian cultural sphere](/wiki/East_Asian_cultural_sphere "East Asian cultural sphere"), particularly in [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Courtesy names are a marker of adulthood and were historically given to men at the age of 20, and sometimes to women upon marriage.

Unlike [art names](/wiki/Art_name "Art name"), which are more akin to [pseudonyms](/wiki/Pseudonym "Pseudonym") or [pen names](/wiki/Pen_name "Pen name"), courtesy names served a formal and respectful purpose.[[1]](#cite_note-Wilkinson-1) In traditional Chinese society, using someone's given name in adulthood was considered disrespectful among peers, making courtesy names essential for formal communication and writing.

Courtesy names often reflect the meaning of the given name or use homophonic characters, and were typically disyllabic after the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty"). The practice also extended to other East Asian cultures, and was sometimes adopted by [Mongols](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols") and [Manchus](/wiki/Manchu_people "Manchu people") during the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"). The choice of a courtesy name was significant, intended to express moral integrity and respect within the cultural context.

## Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Courtesy_name&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Usage")]

A courtesy name is a name traditionally given to Chinese men at the age of 20 [*sui*](/wiki/East_Asian_age_reckoning "East Asian age reckoning"), marking their [coming of age](/wiki/Coming_of_age "Coming of age"). It was sometimes given to women, usually upon marriage.[[1]](#cite_note-Wilkinson-1) The practice is no longer common in modern Chinese society. According to the *[Book of Rites](/wiki/Book_of_Rites "Book of Rites")*, after a man reached adulthood, it was disrespectful for others of the same generation to address him by his [given name](/wiki/Chinese_given_name "Chinese given name").[[3]](#cite_note-Rites-3) Thus, the given name was reserved for oneself and one's elders, whereas the courtesy name would be used by adults of the same generation to refer to one another on formal occasions or in writing. Another translation of *zi* is "style name", but this translation has been criticised as misleading, because it could imply an official or legal title.[[1]](#cite_note-Wilkinson-1)

Generally speaking, courtesy names before the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") were one syllable, and from the Qin to the 20th century they were mostly [disyllabic](/wiki/Disyllabic "Disyllabic"), consisting of two [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character").[[1]](#cite_note-Wilkinson-1) Courtesy names were often relative to the meaning of the person's given name; the relationship could be synonyms, relative affairs, or rarely but sometimes antonym. For example, [Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek")'s given name (中正, [romanized](/wiki/Romanized "Romanized") as Chung-cheng) and courtesy name (介石, romanized as Kai-shek) are both from the *yù* (豫) hexagram 16 of [I Ching](/wiki/I_Ching "I Ching").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Another way to form a courtesy name is to use the homophonic character *zi* (子) – a respectful title for a man – as the first character of the disyllabic courtesy name. Thus, for example, [Gongsun Qiao](/wiki/Gongsun_Qiao "Gongsun Qiao")'s courtesy name was Zichan (子產), and [Du Fu](/wiki/Du_Fu "Du Fu")'s was Zimei (子美). It was also common to construct a courtesy name by using as the first character one which expresses the bearer's birth order among male siblings in his family. Thus [Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius"), whose name was Kong Qiu (孔丘), was given the courtesy name Zhongni (仲尼), where the first character *zhong* indicates that he was the second son born into his family. The characters commonly used are *bo* (伯) for the first, *zhong* (仲) for the second, *shu* (叔) for the third, and *ji* (季) typically for the youngest, if the family consists of more than three sons. General [Sun Jian](/wiki/Sun_Jian "Sun Jian")'s four sons, for instance, were [Sun Ce](/wiki/Sun_Ce "Sun Ce") (伯符, Bófú), [Sun Quan](/wiki/Sun_Quan "Sun Quan") (仲謀, Zhòngmóu), [Sun Yi](/wiki/Sun_Yi "Sun Yi") (叔弼, Shūbì) and [Sun Kuang](/wiki/Sun_Kuang "Sun Kuang") (季佐, Jìzuǒ).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Reflecting a general cultural tendency to [regard names as significant](/wiki/Rectification_of_names "Rectification of names"), the choice of what name to bestow upon one's children was considered very important in traditional China.[[6]](#cite_note-Adamek-6) [Yan Zhitui](/wiki/Yan_Zhitui "Yan Zhitui") of the [Northern Qi](/wiki/Northern_Qi "Northern Qi") dynasty asserted that whereas the purpose of a given name was to distinguish one person from another, a courtesy name should express the bearer's moral integrity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Prior to the twentieth century, [sinicized](/wiki/Sinicization "Sinicization") [Koreans](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), and [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") were also referred to by their courtesy name. The practice was also adopted by some [Mongols](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols") and [Manchus](/wiki/Manchus "Manchus") after the Qing conquest of China.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Courtesy_name&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Examples")]

| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Lǎozǐ](/wiki/Laozi "Laozi") 老子 | Lǐ 李 | Ěr 耳 | Bóyáng 伯陽 |

| Sūnzǐ ([Sun Tzu](/wiki/Sun_Tzu "Sun Tzu")) 孫子 | Sūn 孫 | Wǔ 武 | Chángqīng 長卿 |
| [Cáo Cāo](/wiki/Cao_Cao "Cao Cao") 曹操 | Cáo 曹 | Cāo 操 | Mèngdé 孟德 |
| [Sūn Quán](/wiki/Sun_Quan "Sun Quan") 孫權 | Sūn 孫 | Quán 權 | Zhòngmóu 仲謀 |
| [Guān Yǔ](/wiki/Guan_Yu "Guan Yu") 關羽 | Guān 關 | Yǔ 羽 | Yúncháng 雲長 |
| [Liú Bèi](/wiki/Liu_Bei "Liu Bei") 劉備 | Liú 劉 | Bèi 備 | Xuándé 玄德 |
| [Zhūgé Liàng](/wiki/Zhuge_Liang "Zhuge Liang") 諸葛亮 | Zhūgé 諸葛 | Liàng 亮 | Kǒngmíng 孔明 |
| [Zhào Yún](/wiki/Zhao_Yun "Zhao Yun") 趙雲 | Zhào 趙 | Yún 雲 | Zǐlóng 子龍 |
| [Lǐ Bái](/wiki/Li_Bai "Li Bai") 李白 | Lǐ 李 | Bái 白 | Tàibái 太白 |
| [Sū Dōngpō](/wiki/Su_Shi "Su Shi") 蘇東坡 | Sū 蘇 | Shì 軾 | Zǐzhān 子瞻 |
